mappings.rationaleAI: A core text on communicable disease agents (viruses, bacteria), transmission, vaccination, and the multifactorial concept of contagious disease (A.2, A.4).
Essential for understanding immunity including innate and adaptive immune responses, vaccines, and immunological memory (A.3).
Explores population-level disease patterns, the distinction between endemic and epidemic disease, and disease prevention methods (A.2.b, A.4).
Examines major epidemic diseases -- influenza, plague, cholera, smallpox, tuberculosis, HIV/AIDS -- and their spread and control (A.2.b).
Viruses as major agents of communicable disease in animals and plants, including SARS-CoV-2 and vaccine technology, are the central subject (A.2, B, C).
Bacterial pathogens, antibiotic resistance, and immune system interactions are examined in the context of communicable disease and its control (A.2, A.4, C).
Fungal diseases and parasitism in both plants and animals are explored (B, C).
Disease-causing microorganisms -- including bacteria, fungi, and their ecological roles in disease transmission -- are broadly examined (A.2).
HIV/AIDS illustrates epidemic spread, immunity challenges, prevention, and public health responses as a major communicable disease (A.2.b, A.3, A.4).
A historically significant epidemic disease illustrating endemic and epidemic patterns and public health control measures (A.2.b, A.4).
A major noncommunicable disease involving metabolic defects and environmental hazards, including diagnosis and treatment (A.1, A.4).
Disease prevention and control at the population level, including sanitation and public health infrastructure, are central themes (A.4).
Pharmacological agents used in disease treatment are relevant to the control and treatment of disease (A.4).
Animal diseases, their diagnosis, treatment, and prevention are directly relevant to diseases of non-human animals (C).
